title: A force $\vec{F} = (5\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 2\hat{k})\,\text{N}$ acts on a particle and displaces it from position $\vec{r}_1 = (2\hat{i} + 1\hat{j})\,\text{m}$ to $\vec{r}_2 = (4\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} - 1\hat{k})\,\text{m}$. The work done by the force is:
notes: Work done $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d}$ where $\vec{d} = \vec{r}_2 - \vec{r}_1$.
solution_text: Displacement vector $\vec{d} = \vec{r}_2 - \vec{r}_1 = (4-2)\hat{i} + (3-1)\hat{j} + (-1-0)\hat{k} = 2\hat{i} + 2\hat{j} - \hat{k}$. Work done $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d} = (5\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 2\hat{k}) \cdot (2\hat{i} + 2\hat{j} - \hat{k}) = 5(2) + 3(2) + 2(-1) = 10 + 6 - 2 = 14\,\text{J}$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Performing vector addition instead of subtraction for displacement, or making dot product calculation errors.

title: A force acting on a particle moving along the x-axis is given by $F = (3x^2 + 2x)\,\text{N}$. The work done by this force in displacing the particle from $x=1\,\text{m}$ to $x=3\,\text{m}$ is:
notes: Calculate work by integrating the force: $W = \int_{x_1}^{x_2} F(x) dx$.
common_mistake: Incorrect integration rules or wrong boundary values substitution.

title: A block of mass $m=2\,\text{kg}$ is pulled on a rough horizontal surface ($\mu_k = 0.2$) by a constant force of $10\,\text{N}$. If the block starts from rest, its kinetic energy after moving a distance of $5\,\text{m}$ is:
notes: Apply $W_{\text{net}} = \Delta K$. Net force is $F - f_k$.
solution_text: Normal reaction $N = mg = 20\,\text{N}$. Friction $f_k = \mu_k N = 0.2 \times 20 = 4\,\text{N}$. Net force $F_{\text{net}} = F - f_k = 10 - 4 = 6\,\text{N}$. Net work done $W_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{net}} \times d = 6 \times 5 = 30\,\text{J}$. Since the block starts from rest, the change in kinetic energy is $K_f = 30\,\text{J}$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Forgetting to subtract friction or using the static friction coefficient.

title: The potential energy of a particle moving along the x-axis is given by $U(x) = \frac{a}{x^2} - \frac{b}{x}$, where $a$ and $b$ are positive constants. The equilibrium position of the particle is at $x$ equal to:
notes: At equilibrium, force $F = -dU/dx = 0$.
solution_text: Given $U(x) = ax^{-2} - bx^{-1}$. Force $F = -dU/dx = -(-2ax^{-3} + bx^{-2}) = \frac{2a}{x^3} - \frac{b}{x^2}$. Setting $F = 0 \implies \frac{2a}{x^3} = \frac{b}{x^2} \implies x = \frac{2a}{b}$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Incorrect differentiation of $x^{-2}$ or $x^{-1}$, or forgetting the negative sign in $F = -dU/dx$.

title: A particle is released from height $H$ on a smooth track that ends in a vertical loop of radius $R$. The minimum height $H$ required for the particle to complete the loop without leaving the track is:
notes: The minimum speed at the bottom of the loop to complete circular motion is $v = \sqrt{5gR}$.
solution_text: Using conservation of mechanical energy: initial potential energy $mgH$ is converted to kinetic energy at the bottom of the loop. $mgH = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$. For the particle to complete the loop, the minimum speed at the bottom must be $v = \sqrt{5gR}$. $mgH = \frac{1}{2}m(5gR) \implies H = 2.5R$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Using $v = \sqrt{4gR}$ or $v = \sqrt{2gR}$ as the loop completion velocity.

title: A car of mass $m$ accelerates from rest. The engine delivers constant power $P$. The velocity of the car as a function of time $t$ is proportional to:
notes: Since power is constant, work done is $W = Pt$. By work-energy theorem, $\frac{1}{2}mv^2 = Pt$.
solution_text: Constant power $P = dW/dt \implies W = Pt$. By work-energy theorem, $K = Pt \implies \frac{1}{2}mv^2 = Pt \implies v = \sqrt{\frac{2Pt}{m}} \propto t^{1/2}$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming constant acceleration (which would make $v \propto t$), whereas constant power means acceleration decreases as velocity increases.

title: A body of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ collides elastically head-on with another identical body at rest. After the collision:

options.B: The first body comes to rest and the second body moves with velocity $v$
options.C: The first body rebounds with velocity $-v$

notes: For perfectly elastic head-on collisions between identical masses, the velocities are interchanged.
solution_text: By conservation of momentum and kinetic energy (or using $e=1$), identical mass elastic head-on collisions swap velocities. Initial: $u_1 = v$, $u_2 = 0$. After collision: $v_1 = 0$, $v_2 = v$. So the first comes to rest and the second moves with velocity $v$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming they stick together, which is the condition for a completely inelastic collision.

title: A block of mass $5\,\text{kg}$ is displaced from position $\vec{r}_1 = (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j})\,\text{m}$ to $\vec{r}_2 = (4\hat{i} + 6\hat{j})\,\text{m}$ by a constant force $\vec{F} = (10\hat{i} + 5\hat{j})\,\text{N}$. The work done is ________ $\text{J}$.
solution_text: $\Delta \vec{r} = (4-2)\hat{i} + (6-3)\hat{j} = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}$. $W = \vec{F} \cdot \Delta \vec{r} = (10\hat{i} + 5\hat{j}) \cdot (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}) = 10(2) + 5(3) = 20 + 15 = 35\,\text{J}$.
common_mistake: Adding component magnitudes without using the dot product.

title: A bullet of mass $20\,\text{g}$ is fired with speed $100\,\text{m/s}$ into a wooden block. If the bullet penetrates $20\,\text{cm}$ before coming to rest, the average resistive force exerted by the block is ________ $\text{N}$.
solution_text: Mass $m = 20\,\text{g} = 0.02\,\text{kg}$. Distance $d = 20\,\text{cm} = 0.2\,\text{m}$. Initial kinetic energy $K_i = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} (0.02) (100)^2 = 100\,\text{J}$. Work done by resistive force: $-F \cdot d = -100 \implies F \times 0.2 = 100 \implies F = 500\,\text{N}$.
common_mistake: Failing to convert mass to kg or distance to meters.

title: The potential energy function of a particle is given by $U(x) = x^3 - 3x\,\text{J}$. The coordinate of stable equilibrium for the particle is $x = $ ________ $\text{m}$.
solution_text: $U(x) = x^3 - 3x \implies dU/dx = 3x^2 - 3$. Force $F = -(3x^2 - 3) = 3 - 3x^2 = 0 \implies x^2 = 1 \implies x = \pm 1$. Double derivative $d^2U/dx^2 = 6x$. For stable equilibrium, $d^2U/dx^2 > 0 \implies 6x > 0 \implies x = 1\,\text{m}$.
common_mistake: Reporting $x = -1$ as the stable equilibrium, which is actually unstable ($d^2U/dx^2 = -6 < 0$).

title: A pump is required to lift $600\,\text{kg}$ of water per minute from a well $20\,\text{m}$ deep and eject it with speed $10\,\text{m/s}$. The power of the pump is ________ $\text{W}$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
solution_text: Mass flow rate $\frac{m}{t} = 600\,\text{kg} / 60\,\text{s} = 10\,\text{kg/s}$. Power $P = \frac{dm}{dt} gh + \frac{1}{2} \frac{dm}{dt} v^2 = 10 \times 10 \times 20 + \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 10^2 = 2000 + 500 = 2500\,\text{W}$.
common_mistake: Ignoring the kinetic energy component of the ejected water.

title: A block of mass $m = 1\,\text{kg}$ moving with speed $6\,\text{m/s}$ collides with another block of mass $M = 2\,\text{kg}$ at rest. If the collision is completely inelastic (they stick together), the loss of kinetic energy is ________ $\text{J}$.
solution_text: Common velocity $v_f = \frac{1 \times 6}{1 + 2} = 2\,\text{m/s}$. Initial kinetic energy $K_i = \frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times 6^2 = 18\,\text{J}$. Final kinetic energy $K_f = \frac{1}{2} (1 + 2) v_f^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 2^2 = 6\,\text{J}$. Loss in kinetic energy $= K_i - K_f = 18 - 6 = 12\,\text{J}$.
common_mistake: Using kinetic energy conservation for an inelastic collision.

title: A vertical spring of force constant $k$ is standing on a table. A block of mass $m$ is dropped from a height $h$ above the top of the spring. The maximum compression $x$ of the spring is given by:
notes: Use conservation of mechanical energy: the total loss in gravitational potential energy equals the gain in elastic potential energy.
solution_text: The block falls a total vertical distance of $h + x$ before coming to temporary rest at maximum compression. Therefore, loss in gravitational potential energy is $mg(h+x)$. Gain in spring potential energy is $\frac{1}{2}kx^2$. Equating them: $mg(h+x) = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Using $mgh = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$, neglecting the additional drop distance $x$ when compressing the spring.

title: A block starts sliding from rest down a rough inclined plane of inclination $37^\circ$ and length $5\,\text{m}$. If the coefficient of friction is $0.25$, the work done by gravity is ___ $\text{J}$ and the work done by friction is ___ $\text{J}$ ($m = 2\,\text{kg}, g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
notes: $W_g = mg L \sin\theta$, $W_f = -\mu mg L \cos\theta$.
solution_text: $W_g = mg L \sin\theta = 2 \times 10 \times 5 \times \sin 37^\circ = 100 \times 0.6 = 60\,\text{J}$. $W_f = -f_k \cdot L = -(\mu mg \cos\theta) \cdot L = -(0.25 \times 2 \times 10 \times \cos 37^\circ) \times 5 = -(0.5 \times 10 \times 0.8) \times 5 = -4 \times 5 = -20\,\text{J}$.
common_mistake: Writing work done by friction as positive, or mixing up sine and cosine components.

title: A constant force $\vec{F} = (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j})\,\text{N}$ acts on a body of mass $2\,\text{kg}$. The work done by this force during the first $2\,\text{s}$ starting from rest is ________ $\text{J}$.
notes: Find acceleration vector $\vec{a} = \vec{F}/m$, then displacement vector $\vec{s} = \frac{1}{2}\vec{a}t^2$, then $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}$.
solution_text: Acceleration $\vec{a} = \vec{F}/m = (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j})/2 = \hat{i} + 1.5\hat{j}\,\text{m/s}^2$. Displacement $\vec{s} = \frac{1}{2} \vec{a} t^2 = \frac{1}{2} (\hat{i} + 1.5\hat{j}) \times 4 = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}\,\text{m}$. Work done $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{s} = (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}) \cdot (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j}) = 4 + 9 = 13\,\text{J}$.
common_mistake: Using $1\,\text{kg}$ as mass or forgetting the $1/2$ factor in displacement.

title: A particle of mass $m$ moves along the x-axis with velocity varying as $v = k x^{3/2}$. The work done by all forces in displacing the particle from $x=0$ to $x=d$ is:
notes: Apply the work-energy theorem: $W = \Delta K = \frac{1}{2} m (v_f^2 - v_i^2)$.
solution_text: At $x=0$, velocity $v_i = 0$. At $x=d$, velocity $v_f = k d^{3/2}$. Initial kinetic energy $K_i = 0$. Final kinetic energy $K_f = \frac{1}{2} m v_f^2 = \frac{1}{2} m (k d^{3/2})^2 = \frac{1}{2} m k^2 d^3$. Work done $W = \Delta K = \frac{1}{2} m k^2 d^3$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Integrating the velocity directly or using wrong exponents.

title: For a particle under conservative force, the potential energy is $U(x) = 2x^2 - x^4\,\text{J}$. The coordinate of unstable equilibrium is $x = $ ________ $\text{m}$.
notes: Unstable equilibrium occurs where $dU/dx = 0$ and $d^2U/dx^2 < 0$.
solution_text: $dU/dx = 4x - 4x^3 = 0 \implies 4x(1 - x^2) = 0 \implies x = 0, \pm 1$. $d^2U/dx^2 = 4 - 12x^2$. At $x=0$, $d^2U/dx^2 = 4 > 0$ (stable). At $x=\pm 1$, $d^2U/dx^2 = 4 - 12 = -8 < 0$ (unstable). So coordinate magnitude is 1.
common_mistake: Confusing stable (positive double derivative) with unstable (negative double derivative).

title: A small block slides down a smooth inclined plane of height $h$ and enters a rough horizontal track of length $d$ with friction coefficient $\mu$. If the block comes to rest at the end of the rough track, the relation is:
notes: Apply conservation of mechanical energy and work done by friction: $mgh = f_k d$.
solution_text: The initial energy is potential energy $mgh$. The final energy is 0. The loss is work done by friction on the horizontal surface: $W_f = -\mu mg d$. By energy balance: $mgh - \mu mg d = 0 \implies h = \mu d$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming friction acts on the incline as well, which is described as smooth.

title: An engine lifts a block of mass $100\,\text{kg}$ vertically at a constant speed of $2\,\text{m/s}$. The power of the engine is ________ $\text{W}$ ($g = 10\,\text{m/s}^2$).
notes: Power is force times velocity: $P = F v = mg v$.
solution_text: Force to lift at constant speed $= mg = 100 \times 10 = 1000\,\text{N}$. Power $P = F v = 1000 \times 2 = 2000\,\text{W}$.
common_mistake: Adding kinetic energy or dividing by time unnecessarily.

title: A ball of mass $m$ moving with speed $v$ strikes a wall normally and rebounds with the same speed. The coefficient of restitution is $e=1$. The magnitude of impulse imparted to the wall is:
notes: Impulse is change in momentum of the ball: $I = p_f - p_i = -mv - mv = -2mv$.
solution_text: Initial momentum $= mv$. Final momentum $= -mv$. Change in momentum $= -mv - mv = -2mv$. The magnitude of impulse imparted to the wall is $2mv$. Option B is correct.
common_mistake: Reporting $0$ because the speed is the same, neglecting direction change.

title: A body of mass $m$ collides head-on elastically with another body of mass $M$ at rest. If the colliding body $m$ rebounds with $1/3$ of its initial speed, the ratio of masses $m/M$ is:
notes: Use the elastic collision formula for final velocity $v_1 = \frac{m - M}{m + M} u_1$. Rebound speed is $-u_1 / 3$.
solution_text: We have $v_1 = \frac{m - M}{m + M} u_1$. Since it rebounds with $1/3$ of initial speed: $v_1 = -u_1 / 3$. So $\frac{m - M}{m + M} = -1/3 \implies 3M - 3m = m + M \implies 2M = 4m \implies m/M = 1/2$. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Neglecting the negative sign of rebound velocity, which leads to $m/M = 2$ instead of $1/2$.

title: A ball is dropped from a height of $10\,\text{m}$. It rebounds from the ground with coefficient of restitution $e=0.6$. The height to which the ball rises after the first collision is ________ $\text{m}$.
notes: Height after rebound: $h' = e^2 h$.
solution_text: Velocity before impact $v = \sqrt{2gh}$. Velocity after impact $v' = e v$. Height reached after impact $h' = v'^2 / 2g = e^2 v^2 / 2g = e^2 h$. $h' = 0.6^2 \times 10 = 0.36 \times 10 = 3.6\,\text{m}$.
common_mistake: Multiplying by $e$ instead of $e^2$, yielding 6 m.

title: A particle of mass $m$ is suspended by a string of length $L$. It is given a horizontal velocity at the lowest point. The minimum velocity required for the particle to just reach the highest point (string becomes slack at top) is:
notes: For completing vertical circular motion, the critical velocity at the bottom is $\sqrt{5gL}$.
solution_text: At highest point, string tension $T \ge 0 \implies v_{\text{top}} \ge \sqrt{gL}$. By energy conservation: $K_{\text{bot}} = K_{\text{top}} + mg(2L) \implies \frac{1}{2}mv_{\text{bot}}^2 = \frac{1}{2}mv_{\text{top}}^2 + 2mgL \implies v_{\text{bot}}^2 = v_{\text{top}}^2 + 4gL \ge gL + 4gL = 5gL$. Hence $v_{\text{bot}} \ge \sqrt{5gL}$. Option A is correct.
common_mistake: Using $\sqrt{4gL}$ by forgetting the tension requirement at the top.

title: A body of mass $m$ starting from rest is accelerated by a force that delivers constant power $P$. The displacement of the body in time $t$ is proportional to:
notes: Velocity $v = c t^{1/2}$ where $c$ is a constant. Displacement $s = \int v dt \propto t^{3/2}$.
solution_text: From constant power: $v = \sqrt{\frac{2P}{m}} t^{1/2}$. Displacement $s = \int v dt = \int \sqrt{\frac{2P}{m}} t^{1/2} dt = \frac{2}{3} \sqrt{\frac{2P}{m}} t^{3/2} \propto t^{3/2}$. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Thinking $s \propto t^2$, which only holds for constant force/acceleration.

title: A particle moves along a curve under a force $\vec{F} = (3x^2\hat{i} + 2y\hat{j})\,\text{N}$. The work done in moving it from $(0,0)$ to $(2,3)$ is ________ $\text{J}$.
notes: Integrate components: $W = \int F_x dx + \int F_y dy = \int_0^2 3x^2 dx + \int_0^3 2y dy$.
common_mistake: Adding the coordinates first or multiplying components incorrectly.

title: A ball of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ collides obliquely with an identical ball at rest. If the collision is perfectly elastic ($e=1$), the angle between their velocities after collision is:
notes: For elastic collision of identical masses, the final velocity vectors are perpendicular if the target is initially at rest.
solution_text: By conservation of momentum: $\vec{v} = \vec{v}_1 + \vec{v}_2 \implies v^2 = v_1^2 + v_2^2 + 2\vec{v}_1 \cdot \vec{v}_2$. By conservation of kinetic energy (since elastic): $v^2 = v_1^2 + v_2^2$. Equating these gives $\vec{v}_1 \cdot \vec{v}_2 = 0$. Hence the angle between final velocity vectors is $90^\circ$. Option C is correct.
common_mistake: Assuming they travel together along the same line (which only happens for head-on collisions).
